title-group.article-title: Effects of different exercise modalities on lipid profiles in overweight and obese children and adolescents: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This study aimed to systematically compare, through a network meta-analysis (NMA), the effects of different exercise modalities—including aerobic exercise (AE), resistance training (RT), high-intensity interval training (HIIT), and combined training (COM)—on the lipid profile of overweight/obese children and adolescents.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The study followed the PRISMA-NMA guidelines and systematically searched randomized controlled trials (RCTs) published from 1999 to August 31, 2025 in the PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, Web of Science, and EBSCO databases. A total of 35 RCTs (n = 1601 participants) were included. Random-effects models in Stata 16.0 were applied to calculate pooled effect sizes with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Network meta-analysis was performed to synthesize and analyze the data, and intervention effects were ranked using the Surface Under the Cumulative Ranking (SUCRA) curve.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The findings revealed differential effects of exercise modalities on lipid regulation. RT ranked highest for reducing total cholesterol (TC). AE was the only intervention that significantly increased high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C). HIIT demonstrated the greatest efficacy in lowering low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglycerides (TG). COM showed no superior effect for any single outcome among the four indicators. From an integrated perspective, AE intervention was most effective in elevating HDL-C but relatively weak in reducing LDL-C. Conversely, although RT and HIIT were less effective for raising HDL-C, they exhibited the strongest effects in reducing LDL-C. Compared with the no-exercise control group (CON), resistance training (RT) and high-intensity interval training (HIIT) significantly reduced total cholesterol (TC) levels in individuals with overweight or obesity. Aerobic exercise (AE) significantly increased HDL-C levels in this population. HIIT and combined exercise (COM) significantly reduced LDL-C levels, while HIIT, AE, and COM significantly lowered triglyceride (TG) levels.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The findings suggest that different exercise modalities may exert differential effects on specific lipid parameters. These distinct patterns offer preliminary support for phenotype-targeted exercise prescription, providing an evidence base for refining clinical guidelines and enhancing the precision of cardiovascular health interventions.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s13102-026-01544-9.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: According to projections from the World Obesity Atlas 2024, the number of children and adolescents aged 5–19 years who are overweight or obese is expected to exceed 750 million worldwide by 2035 [1]. Obesity not only affects physical development in children and adolescents (e.g., musculoskeletal system and respiratory function), but also markedly increases the risk of dyslipidemia. Moreover, evidence suggests that elevated lipid levels during puberty may predict dyslipidemia in adulthood——the tracking correlation coefficients for low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, total cholesterol, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, and triglycerides were 0.55–0.65, 0.51–0.65, 0.46–0.57, and 0.32–0.40, respectively [2–4]. In a study conducted in Denmark involving 958 children and adolescents (median age 11.9 years) with a BMI at or above the 90th percentile, the prevalence of dyslipidemia was 38.0% in the overweight/obesity group, which was significantly higher than the 10.8% observed in the normal-weight control group [5], making obesity the most common cause of dyslipidemia in the pediatric population [6–8]. Dyslipidemia generally refers to abnormal levels of triglycerides (TG) and/or apolipoproteins in the blood, resulting in alterations in one or more lipid components. Its features include elevated levels of certain components (e.g., low-density lipoprotein cholesterol [LDL-C]) or reduced levels of others (e.g., high-density lipoprotein cholesterol [HDL-C]) [9–13], which collectively promote the development of atherosclerosis [14]. These findings indicate that lipid metabolism disorders in obese individuals carry potential long-term risks, with cumulative effects possibly emerging early in life. Therefore, early monitoring and timely intervention for lipid profiles in obese populations are of great significance [15].

Currently, clinical guidelines regard lipid-lowering agents (e.g., statins) as the first-line treatment for dyslipidemia [8]. These drugs can improve lipid profiles to some extent and are generally considered safe, with common side effects including myalgia (1–10%), rare rhabdomyolysis (< 0.1%), and reversible elevations in liver enzymes (~ 1%) [16]. While statins appear safe for most patients, however, because adolescents are in a critical period of growth and development, the long-term effects of pharmacological treatments on hormonal metabolism and skeletal development remain unclear. For example, one study reported that adolescents in the simvastatin treatment group exhibited significantly reduced levels of dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEA-S) compared with the placebo group, and DHEA-S is an important precursor for androgen synthesis during puberty [17]. Therefore, the use of such medications requires careful evaluation. Their long-term impact on hormone metabolism and skeletal development in adolescents—who are at a critical stage of growth—remains unclear, necessitating cautious evaluation [17, 18]. Furthermore, pharmacological treatment rarely addresses the multidimensional health issues associated with adolescent obesity, such as low cardiorespiratory fitness, reduced muscular strength, and psychological risks. Once medication is discontinued, lipid levels may rebound [17]. Exercise, as a low-cost lifestyle intervention measure, is recommended for improving blood lipid and lipoprotein levels [19], which can fundamentally solve the problem of dyslipidemia and has become the cornerstone of the treatment of dyslipidemia in children [6, 20]. An early review published in 2001 indicated that exercise can moderately increase high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) and reduce triglycerides (TG) [21]. This finding is consistent with several studies, which reported that expending 900 kcal per week or exercising for 120 min could significantly improve HDL-C and TG [22, 23]. Kodama et al. [24] further confirmed that exercise training produced modest improvements in HDL-C, but these effects were more pronounced in populations with body mass index < 28 kg/m² and baseline TC < 220 mg/dL.

In terms of comparative effects of different exercise modalities, a meta-analysis suggested that combined training improved TG and TC levels, while AE significantly increased HDL-C; however, no statistically significant effect on LDL-C was observed across all exercise modalities [25]. In contrast, another meta-analysis reported that AE significantly reduced LDL-C levels but had no marked effects on other parameters [26]. Moreover, other studies argued that exercise may not directly reduce LDL-C but rather improve lipid profiles by inducing favorable changes in LDL-C particle size [27]. The evidence in obese children remains inconsistent. One review suggested that AE had a pronounced effect on TG concentrations, while COM contributed to HDL-C regulation [14]. Similarly, a meta-analysis including 25 studies found that AE significantly reduced TG, but showed no significant changes in TC, HDL-C, or LDL-C [28].This study focuses on the effects of different exercise modalities on the lipid profile of obese children. These inconsistencies may stem from differences in population characteristics, variations in exercise intervention parameters (such as type and dose), and methodological heterogeneity.Although dietary control and physical activity are often jointly recommended as primary strategies for obesity intervention [29], evidence suggests that increasing physical activity alone—without concomitant energy intake restriction—can effectively improve multiple health indicators, including body composition [30], cardiometabolic parameters, and cardiorespiratory fitness [31, 32]. To ensure high internal validity and enable a “clean” isolation of exercise effects across different modalities, we elected to exclude participants who simultaneously received structured dietary interventions. We explicitly acknowledge the trade-off between this rigorous control and external validity, as real-world clinical management often combines exercise with dietary changes. However, this exclusion was strictly necessary to precisely quantify the independent efficacy of each exercise modality without the confounding influence of caloric restriction.

Given that traditional meta-analyses are constrained in their ability to compare multiple interventions within a single analytic framework, whereas network meta-analysis (NMA) can integrate indirect evidence and provide simultaneous comparisons and ranking of all interventions, we adopted an NMA approach to systematically evaluate the effects of different exercise modalities. This method enables clarification of their relative effectiveness and facilitates the identification of optimal strategies.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This study followed the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [33]. The review was registered with PROSPERO under the registration number 2025 CRD420251122967 (https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/PROSPERO/view/CRD420251122967).

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: We searched PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, Web of Science, and EBSCO databases using a combination of MeSH and Emtree terms and keywords. The search was restricted to English-language randomized controlled trials (RCTs) published from 1999 to August 31, 2025. The complete search strategies for each database are provided in Appendix 2.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: This study adhered to the Participants, Intervention, Comparator, Outcomes, and Study design (PICOS) framework, with the following criteria:

Participants: Obesity and overweight children and adolescents (≤ 18 years). This study adopted the World Health Organization (WHO) growth reference standards for children and adolescents aged 5–19 years, which provide age- and sex-specific BMI percentiles, criteria as BMI ≥ 95th percentile, and overweight as BMI ≥ 85th percentile [34, 35]. For studies without reported BMI percentiles, obesity was defined as BMI ≥ 25 kg/m² or BMI z-score ≥ 1 [36]. We explicitly acknowledge that applying adult-derived fixed cutoffs to pediatric populations limits precision and may introduce classification bias; however, this criterion was adopted only when age- and sex-specific percentile data were unavailable.

list-item.p: Intervention: At least one experimental group was required, with all intervention groups receiving a structured exercise program lasting ≥4 weeks. Because the core component of COM remains exercise, aligning with the objective of this study to compare the effects of different modalities within the exercise domain. Eligible exercise modalities included aerobic exercise (AE), resistance training (RT), high-intensity interval training (HIIT), and combined training (COM).

Comparator: The control group consisted of participants who did not receive any structured exercise intervention or who engaged only in light stretching. We pooled these conditions because light stretching poses negligible metabolic demand and does not reach the intensity threshold required to induce cardiovascular or lipid adaptations. Consequently, both conditions are widely considered equivalent non-training comparators in pediatric exercise science literature.Participants were required to maintain their habitual lifestyle and physical activity patterns and were not permitted to engage in any additional organized exercise during the study period.Studies without a traditional control group but with direct comparisons between different exercise interventions were also included.

Outcomes:We designated the complete lipid profile as the primary interest of this study. Specifically, total cholesterol (TC), triglycerides (TG), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) were all defined as co-primary endpoints. This designation reflects the fact that comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment in pediatric populations requires the integrated evaluation of all four parameters rather than reliance on a single marker.Studies reporting only other biomarkers (e.g., fasting insulin, adiponectin) were excluded.
Study design: Only RCTs were included to ensure high-level evidence.

Exclusion criteria: Studies were excluded if they met any of the following: (i) non-English publications; (ii) dissertations, conference abstracts, or records without full text; (iii) Studies with incomplete or missing outcome data for which the necessary information could not be obtained before data lock were excluded. This exclusion was applied only after at least two attempts to contact the corresponding author (spaced 2–4 weeks apart, spanning a total contact window of approximately 6 weeks) yielded no response; (iv) duplicate publications; (v) studies involving special populations (e.g., genetic lipid metabolism disorders, severe metabolic syndrome, or major comorbidities); (vi) studies involving dietary interventions or nutritional supplementation (e.g., AE combined with dietary restriction), to isolate the independent effect of exercise on lipid profiles.

sec.title: Literature screening
sec.p: All retrieved studies were imported into EndNote X9 to remove duplicates. Two independent reviewers (QZL and XYX) screened titles and abstracts to identify potentially eligible studies. Full texts were retrieved when necessary, and corresponding authors were contacted for missing data. Studies meeting the inclusion criteria were independently verified and assessed by two reviewers (QZL and XYX) to ensure that all duplicates were thoroughly removed.Discrepancies were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer (JX).

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data extraction was independently performed by two researchers (QZL and BJZ) using standardized forms developed in accordance with the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions. To minimize subjective bias, the extraction process was conducted without deliberate blinding to author names, affiliations, or journal titles, as such information could not be concealed during the abstract screening stage. Nevertheless, all extraction procedures strictly adhered to predefined objective criteria. Extractors focused on study design, participant characteristics, intervention details, and outcome data.

After extraction, the two researchers independently cross-checked the results. Any discrepancies were resolved through re-examination of the original articles and discussion, with arbitration by a senior reviewer (JX) when necessary.

This systematic review primarily focused on the effectiveness of exercise interventions on lipid profiles. As the original studies generally did not systematically or consistently report adverse events (e.g., musculoskeletal injuries), safety outcomes were not included as predefined components for extraction or analysis.

Extracted data included: (1) first author; (2) publication year; (3) intervention details (sample size, sex, age, BMI, and body weight for each group); (4) intervention characteristics (exercise modality, duration, frequency, and session length); (5) outcomes (pre- and post-intervention values for experimental and control groups). When necessary data were unavailable, corresponding authors were contacted; studies were excluded if data could not be obtained; (6) Compliance; (7) Source of funds.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment for included studies
sec.p: Risk of bias was assessed independently by two reviewers (QZL and BJZ) using the original Cochrane Collaboration’s Risk of Bias tool (RoB 1) as described by Higgins et al. [37]. We selected RoB 1 to ensure methodological comparability with previous reviews in the field of pediatric exercise and to appropriately accommodate the reporting standards of older trials included in our dataset (dating back to 1999).The assessment covered random sequence generation, allocation concealment, blinding of participants and personnel, blinding of outcome assessors, incomplete outcome data, selective reporting, and other potential biases. For the “incomplete outcome data” risk-of-bias domain, we prespecified that studies with a loss-to-follow-up or dropout rate exceeding 20%, and without the use of intention-to-treat analysis to address the missing data, would be judged as having a high risk of bias for this item. Disagreements were resolved by a third reviewer (JX).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Inconsistency within closed loops was evaluated by calculating inconsistency factors and their 95% confidence intervals (CIs) [38]. Inconsistency models were used for testing; when P>0.05, consistency models were applied [39]. Consistency between direct and indirect evidence was assessed using the node-splitting method. This approach evaluates only those comparisons that form closed loops within the evidence network—specifically, cases in which two interventions are compared both directly and indirectly through a common comparator. A p-value greater than 0.05 indicates no significant inconsistency between the direct and indirect evidence within that loop. Ranking of intervention effects was conducted using the Surface Under the Cumulative Ranking (SUCRA) curve [40]. SUCRA values range from 0 to 100, where 100 indicates the most effective intervention and 0 the least effective [41]. Publication bias and small-study effects were examined using funnel plots.

Effect sizes were pooled using pre- to post-intervention changes in means and standard deviations (SDs), accounting for baseline differences. Change SDs were calculated according to formulas from the Cochrane Systematic Review Guidelines (version 6.3) [42]. For studies that did not directly report the standard deviation of change scores, we estimated this value following the guidelines of the Cochrane Handbook (version 6.3). Specifically, following standard practice outlined in the Cochrane Handbook, we assumed a conservative correlation coefficient of r = 0.5 between baseline and post-intervention measurements to impute missing standard deviations of change scores.This study followed the PRISMA-NMA guidelines [43]. Analyses were conducted within a frequentist framework [44], applying random-effects models in Stata 16.0 to calculate pooled effect sizes and 95% CIs. The choice of this model was prespecified and accounted for the potential heterogeneity across the included studies. We report τ² (the estimated between-study variance) as the primary indicator of heterogeneity. Standardized mean differences (SMDs) were used as effect measures due to heterogeneity in measurement units across outcomes. A network plot was generated to visualize the evidence structure among the different exercise modalities. In this plot, each node represents an intervention (or the control group), with its size proportional to the total number of participants assigned to that intervention across all included studies. Edges between nodes indicate the presence of direct comparisons, and the thickness of each edge reflects the number of studies contributing to that comparison. SUCRA curves were used to rank intervention effectiveness [40]. The SUCRA value ranges from 0 to 100, with 100 indicating the most effective intervention and 0 indicating the least effective one [41]. Funnel plots were applied to detect publication bias and small-sample effects.

For studies that directly compared two exercise modalities (e.g., A vs. B) without a shared control group, we followed the Cochrane Handbook guidelines and extracted the post-intervention means, standard deviations, and sample sizes from both groups to calculate the standardized mean difference as the effect size for the direct comparison. This effect size represents the direct relative effect between interventions A and B.All studies were incorporated into a single evidence network through shared intervention nodes to enable network meta-analysis.

For multi-arm trials involving a shared control group, we treated the data structure within a multivariate random-effects meta-analysis framework. Instead of simply splitting the arms into independent pairwise comparisons (which would lead to double-counting), we explicitly modeled the within-study correlations by incorporating the covariance structure of the effect sizes. This was implemented using the Stata network package (version 1.2.1) by defining unique study identifiers, which automatically adjusts standard errors to account for the interdependence of comparisons derived from the same trial.

To provide an integrated assessment of the combined effects of exercise interventions on key lipid parameters, we generated cluster-ranking plots to jointly display the effects of each intervention on high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C). This combination was selected because these two biomarkers represent core targets in cardiovascular risk assessment with opposing clinical implications, and exercise interventions may influence them heterogeneously. Such analyses help identify exercise modalities that best achieve the desirable profile of “increasing HDL-C while lowering LDL-C.”

Given the limited number of studies under certain intervention nodes in the network meta-analysis, conducting formal subgroup analyses or meta-regression could yield unstable or misleading results due to insufficient statistical power. Therefore, no hypothesis-driven subgroup analyses or meta-regressions were performed. To facilitate interpretation and provide context for potential sources of heterogeneity, we comprehensively reported study characteristics: in the supplementary materials, a complete table details each study’s sample size, age range, sex distribution, baseline BMI, specific intervention parameters (frequency, intensity, duration), and follow-up period.

sec.title: Literature screening process
sec.p: Study Selection and Characteristics The database search identified 2,162 records (PubMed n = 656, Embase n = 568, Cochrane n = 402, Web of Science n = 369, EBSCO n = 167). Following duplicate removal (n = 925) and hierarchical screening, 35 RCTs (n = 1,601 participants) were included (Fig. 1). Primary reasons for exclusion at the full-text stage included ineligible interventions (n = 115), special populations (n = 61), and missing outcome data (n = 49) (Appendix 4).

caption.p: Flow chart of the studies selection process

sec.title: Basic characteristics of included studies
sec.p: Thirty-five RCTs (see Appendix 5) published between 1999 and 2025 were included, covering diverse regions across the Americas (Brazil, United States, Canada), Asia (China, Korea, Iran, Singapore, Thailand), Europe (Switzerland, Turkey, Spain), and Oceania (Australia), providing strong international representation. A total of 1601 participants aged ≤ 18 years were included. The overall age range of participants was 7 to 18 years, with mean ages ranging from 10 to 16 years, spanning various stages of puberty. Most studies enrolled mixed-sex samples, while 15 studies included only one sex. Among the 35 included studies, 29 (82.9%) reported all four lipid parameters (TC, HDL-C, LDL-C, and TG). The remaining six studies (17.1%) reported only a subset of these indicators: three studies reported HDL-C, LDL-C, and TG; two studies reported HDL-C and TG; and one study reported TC alone. All available data were included in the corresponding meta-analyses for each lipid outcome. Exercise modalities included AE, RT, HIIT, COM, and controls (CON). Intervention durations ranged from 4 weeks to 12 months, with 12 weeks being most common. Training frequencies ranged from 2 to 6 sessions per week, typically 3–5 sessions/week, and session lengths ranged from 30 to 90 min. Intensities were prescribed using objective physiological indices, including 50%–85% of peak oxygen uptake (VO₂peak), 55%–95% of maximum heart rate (HRmax), 35%–90% of heart rate reserve (HRR), and 40%–70% of one-repetition maximum (1RM). Some studies additionally applied ventilatory threshold (VT1), maximal aerobic speed (MAS), or peak power output (PPO) as training load indicators. To maximize the retention of available evidence and ensure the stability of the network geometry, risk-of-bias assessments were not used as exclusion criteria. Furthermore, since no studies were classified as “low quality” (overall high risk) and excluding the limited number of moderate-quality studies (n = 7) could compromise network connectivity and statistical power in sparse nodes, we adhered to our pre-specified protocol and specific sensitivity analyses based on quality were not conducted.

sec.title: Quality assessment results
sec.p: Risk of bias assessment results are shown in Fig. 2, with detailed results in Supplementary Material 6. Overall, 35 studies reported randomization procedures, and 34 reported allocation concealment. Only one study implemented double-blinding, and two used blinded outcome assessment. Attrition was reported in 30 studies; attrition > 20% was considered high risk unless intention-to-treat analyses were conducted. Seven studies reported attrition rates > 20%. No studies were rated high risk due to small sample sizes (< 10 participants per group), lack of supervision, or conflicts of interest.Based on previously adapted criteria [45], studies were classified as follows: high quality—no high-risk domains and ≤ 3 unclear domains (n = 28); moderate quality—1 high-risk domain or ≥ 4 unclear domains (n = 7); low quality—other cases (n = 0).

caption.p: Distribution of the methodological quality of the included studies. The percentages shown in the figure represent the proportion of included studies judged to have each corresponding risk level (high, moderate, or low)

sec.title: The effect of different interventions on TC
sec.p: Figure 3 provides the network plot, league table, and SUCRA plot for the effects of different interventions on TC. Figure 3A shows the SUCRA rankings of the five interventions, where the area under the curve represents the effectiveness of each intervention; a larger area indicates a higher ranking. For lowering TC, the ranking was: RT > HIIT > COM > AE > CON.As shown in Fig. 3B, among all exercise modalities, aerobic exercise (AE) appeared most frequently, whereas resistance training (RT) was least frequent. Supplementary Material 7 presents the contributions of direct and indirect comparisons to the NMA and the number of studies for each direct comparison. Figure 3C summarizes the estimated pairwise effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) among the five interventions. Compared with the no-exercise group (CON), the following interventions significantly reduced TC in overweight or obese individuals: RT [SMD = -0.43, 95% CI (-0.79, -0.06), P < 0.05] and HIIT [SMD = -0.31, 95% CI (-0.53, -0.08), P < 0.05]. By contrast, COM [SMD = -0.17, 95% CI (-0.38, 0.04), P > 0.05] and AE [SMD = -0.09, 95% CI (-0.23, 0.06), P > 0.05] did not show significant differences.

caption.p: Network Meta-Analysis of TC: SUCRA Plot, Network Plot and League Table. A illustrates the SUCRA Plot, where the size of the area under the curve indicates the effectiveness of each intervention. B presents the Network Plot. Note: Node size is proportional to total participants, and edge thickness reflects the number of studies. C displays the pairwise comparison League Table, where the estimated effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) represent the difference between the intervention on the top and the intervention on the right. *Indicates that the comparison is based on indirect evidence

sec.title: The effect of different interventions on HDL-C
sec.p: Figure 4 provides the network plot, league table, and SUCRA plot for the effects of different interventions on HDL-C. Figure 4A shows the SUCRA rankings of the five interventions; a larger area under the curve indicates a higher ranking. For improving HDL-C, the ranking was: AE > COM > HIIT > RT > CON.As shown in Fig. 4B, among all exercise modalities, AE appeared most frequently, whereas RT was least frequent. Supplementary Material 7 presents the contributions of direct and indirect comparisons to the NMA and the number of studies for each direct comparison. Figure 4C summarizes the estimated pairwise effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) among the five interventions. Compared with CON, AE significantly increased HDL-C in overweight or obese individuals [SMD = 0.28, 95% CI (0.14, 0.43), P < 0.05]. By contrast, COM [SMD = 0.15, 95% CI (-0.04, 0.33), P > 0.05], HIIT [SMD = 0.06, 95% CI (-0.16, 0.29), P > 0.05], and RT [SMD = 0.04, 95% CI (-0.34, 0.41), P > 0.05] did not show significant differences.

caption.p: Network Meta-Analysis of HDL-C: SUCRA Plot, Network Plot and League Table. A illustrates the SUCRA Plot, where the size of the area under the curve indicates the effectiveness of each intervention. B presents the Network Plot. Note: Node size is proportional to total participants, and edge thickness reflects the number of studies. C displays the pairwise comparison League Table, where the estimated effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) represent the difference between the intervention on the top and the intervention on the right. *Indicates that the comparison is based on indirect evidence

sec.title: The effect of different interventions on LDL-C
sec.p: Figure 5 provides the network plot, league table, and SUCRA plot for LDL-C. Figure 5A shows the SUCRA rankings; a larger area indicates a higher ranking. For lowering LDL-C, the ranking was: HIIT > RT > COM > AE > CON.As shown in Fig. 5B, AE appeared most frequently, whereas RT was least frequent. Supplementary Material 7 presents the contributions of direct and indirect comparisons to the NMA and the number of studies for each direct comparison. Figure 5C summarizes the estimated pairwise effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) among the five interventions. Compared with CON, HIIT [SMD = -0.39, 95% CI (-0.70, -0.07), P < 0.05] and COM [SMD = -0.29, 95% CI (-0.56, -0.03), P < 0.05] significantly reduced LDL-C, whereas RT [SMD = -0.42, 95% CI (-0.98, 0.15), P > 0.05] and AE [SMD = -0.12, 95% CI (-0.33, 0.09), P > 0.05] did not.

caption.p: Network Meta-Analysis of LDL-C: SUCRA Plot, Network Plot and League Table.A illustrates the SUCRA Plot, where the size of the area under the curve indicates the effectiveness of each intervention. B presents the Network Plot. Note: Node size is proportional to total participants, and edge thickness reflects the number of studies. C displays the pairwise comparison League Table, where the estimated effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) represent the difference between the intervention on the top and the intervention on the right. *Indicates that the comparison is based on indirect evidence

sec.title: The effect of different interventions on TG
sec.p: Figure 6 provides the network plot, league table, and SUCRA plot for TG. Figure 6A shows the SUCRA rankings; a larger area indicates a higher ranking. For lowering TG, the ranking was: HIIT > AE > COM > RT > CON.

caption.p: Network Meta-Analysis of TG: SUCRA Plot ,Network Plot and League Table. A illustrates the SUCRA Plot, where the size of the area under the curve indicates the effectiveness of each intervention. B presents the Network Plot. The size of each node is proportional to the total number of participants assigned to that intervention, and the thickness of the lines corresponds to the number of available studies. C displays the pairwise comparison League Table, where the estimated effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) represent the difference between the intervention on the top and the intervention on the right. *Indicates that the comparison is based on indirect evidence

sec.p: As shown in Fig. 6B, AE appeared most frequently, whereas RT was least frequent. Supplementary Material 7 presents the contributions of direct and indirect comparisons to the NMA and the number of studies for each direct comparison.

Figure 6C summarizes the estimated pairwise effect size differences (SMD with 95% CI) among the five interventions. Compared with CON, HIIT [SMD = -0.76, 95% CI (-1.26, -0.25), P < 0.05], AE [SMD = -0.53, 95% CI (-0.88, -0.18), P < 0.05], and COM [SMD = -0.50, 95% CI (-0.93, -0.07), P < 0.05] significantly reduced TG, whereas RT did not [SMD = -0.33, 95% CI (-1.31, 0.65), P > 0.05].

sec.title: Inconsistency assessment
sec.p: Evaluation of inconsistency using local (loop-specific), global, and node-splitting approaches revealed no significant inconsistency between direct and indirect evidence across any lipid outcomes (all P > 0.05). Detailed statistical outputs for all inconsistency tests are provided in Supplementary Material (Appendix 9).

Appendix 10 provides the forest plots for lipid profile comparisons. To clearly present the direct comparison evidence that underpins the network structure, we also include in Supplementary Material (Appendix 10) the forest plots for all pairwise comparisons. These plots visually display the final pairwise results derived from the network meta-analysis, illustrating the standardized mean differences and their 95% confidence intervals based on network mixed estimates that integrate both direct and indirect evidence.

sec.title: Pooled effect size analysis and ranking results
sec.p: Funnel plots for TC, HDL-C, and LDL-C outcomes appeared relatively symmetrical, suggesting a low likelihood of publication bias or small-study effects. By contrast, the TG funnel plot showed some asymmetry. Visual inspection indicated that the regression line displayed a clear slope rather than a horizontal line, suggesting potential small-sample effects or publication bias (Fig. 7). It should be noted that due to the limited statistical power and methodological complexity of conducting formal bias tests (e.g., Egger’s test) within a network meta-analysis framework, we relied on qualitative visual assessment rather than formal hypothesis testing (P-values).

caption.p: Network meta-analysis funnel plots for Adipokines. A：TC，B：HDL-C，C：LDL-C，D：TG

sec.title: The effect of different interventions on HDL-C + LDL-C
sec.p: To provide an integrated assessment of the combined effects of each intervention on multiple key lipid outcomes, we performed a cluster-ranking analysis. Specifically, we selected two core biomarkers with opposing pathophysiological directions—high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C)—and used their SUCRA values. Each intervention was treated as a data point plotted on a two-dimensional plane, with the SUCRA value for HDL-C on the x-axis and that for LDL-C on the y-axis. In this coordinate space, interventions located closer to the upper-right quadrant indicate superior overall performance in simultaneously increasing HDL-C and reducing LDL-C. Cluster ranking plots of interventions on HDL-C and LDL-C are presented in Fig. 8. AE was most effective for improving HDL-C but relatively weak for reducing LDL-C. RT and HIIT were less effective in improving HDL-C but ranked highest for LDL-C reduction. COM demonstrated balanced, moderate effects on both HDL-C and LDL-C. By contrast, CON performed poorest in both outcomes.

caption.p: The Clustered Ranking Plot of HDL-C + LDL-C. The horizontal axis represents the effect on increasing HDL-C, and the vertical axis represents the effect on reducing LDL-C, with points in the top right representing the best effects, in this two-dimensional ranking, the upper-right quadrant represents the most favorable overall effect, where the SUCRA values for both increased high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) and decreased low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) are highest

sec.title: Heterogeneity assessment
sec.p: All networks were synthesized using random-effects models. The estimated heterogeneity variances (τ²) for each outcome network are presented in Table S1. Specifically, the τ² values for total cholesterol (TC) and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) were very low (0.009 and 0.015, respectively), indicating a high level of consistency across the contributing evidence. The low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) network exhibited moderate heterogeneity (τ² = 0.131). Notably, the τ² value for the triglyceride (TG) network was high (0.566), suggesting substantial variability in effect sizes across studies. This heterogeneity is likely attributable to the distinct variations in intervention protocols observed across the included trials, specifically the wide range of intervention durations (4 weeks to 12 months) and varying exercise intensities (e.g., 40% vs. 75% 1RM for RT).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This network meta-analysis systematically evaluated the independent effects of distinct exercise modalities on pediatric lipid profiles. Our findings suggest phenotype-specific advantages: RT appeared most effective for lowering TC, AE for elevating HDL-C, and HIIT for reducing LDL-C and TG.Although HIIT was not ranked first in any single SUCRA metric, it was observed to perform significantly better than the no-exercise control in reducing LDL-C and TG, while also showing favorable trends in increasing HDL-C and reducing TC. This broad and synergistic pattern of improvement suggests that HIIT may be considered a valuable intervention option in clinical settings aiming for comprehensive management of dyslipidemia or overall cardiometabolic health.

To contextualize the clinical relevance of the statistically significant findings, effect sizes were interpreted using Cohen’s *d* criteria, where standardized mean differences (SMDs) of ~ 0.2, 0.5, and 0.8 generally correspond to small, medium, and large effects, respectively.HIIT showed a pronounced reduction in triglycerides (TG) (SMD = − 0.76), approaching a large effect, which may indicate a meaningful modulation of elevated TG commonly seen in adolescents with obesity. Both the effect of RT on total cholesterol (TC) (SMD = − 0.43) and of HIIT on LDL-C (SMD = − 0.39) fell within the medium range, supporting their potential relevance for improving atherogenic lipids. Although AE induced only a small increase in HDL-C (SMD = 0.28), the positive change remains physiologically notable given the cardioprotective role of HDL-C.

Given the lack of established minimal clinically important differences (MCIDs) for pediatric lipids, we rely on standardized effect sizes (Cohen’s d) as a quantitative proxy for efficacy. While serving as a valuable relative gauge, these estimates should be interpreted cautiously alongside individual baseline risks rather than as definitive targets. Practically, for youth with specific lipid abnormalities, prioritizing modalities demonstrating larger effect sizes for the relevant biomarkers could be considered to optimize metabolic outcomes.

With respect to TC reduction, RT ranked first, followed by HIIT, COM, and AE, while CON showed the weakest improvement. We hypothesize that the resistance component within combined training likely contributes to these effects through distinct metabolic pathways [25]. However, isolating the specific additive versus synergistic contributions of RT requires verification through future studies utilizing rigorous factorial designs.

Studies in adults suggest that RT enhances lecithin–cholesterol acyltransferase (L-CAT) activity, facilitating reverse cholesterol transport [46]. While offering a plausible physiological framework, we acknowledge that extrapolating these adult-derived findings to pediatric populations requires caution due to developmental differences. Thus, this specific mechanism remains a hypothesis warranting empirical testing in adolescents [47].

In contrast, the effect of AE on reducing TC was comparatively weaker. We speculate that this may be related to the generally low intervention intensity used in the included studies. Most AE interventions for children involved low- to moderate-intensity activities such as walking or jogging, which may not have reached the metabolic threshold required to induce meaningful lipid improvements. Evidence in adults indicates that exercise intensity must reach ~ 70% of VO₂max to significantly reduce TC [48]. However, it remains uncertain whether this specific threshold applies strictly to children, given their distinct physiological responses to exercise stress compared to adults. Future trials should directly validate this dose-response relationship in pediatric cohorts.

Therefore, we suggest that future research in adolescents should place greater emphasis on increasing the intensity of aerobic exercise to fully leverage its potential for lipid improvement. However, this hypothesis requires validation through randomized controlled trials that directly compare AE interventions of different intensities.

Regarding HDL-C elevation, AE ranked highest, followed by COM and HIIT, with RT showing limited effects. This finding aligns with existing literature. For instance, studies testing athletes after completing a 42 km race walk found a near doubling of lipoprotein lipase activity, suggesting that such exercise sharply enhances enzyme activity. This increase in lipoprotein lipase activity may mediate both reductions in serum TG and subsequent elevations in HDL-C [49]. Ferguson [50] proposed an energy expenditure threshold capable of inducing lipoprotein profile changes, reporting that moderate-intensity exercise expending 1100–1500 kcal at ~ 70% VO₂ had greater effects on HDL-C. Furthermore, long-duration cycling as a representative form of moderate-to-vigorous endurance-type aerobic exercise has been shown to enhance lipoprotein lipase (LPL) activity, accelerate VLDL-C catabolism, and promote the conversion of HDL-C3 into HDL-C2, which exerts stronger cardioprotective effects. Simultaneously, reductions in TG decrease apoA-I catabolism, while reduced cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP) activity diminishes HDL-C2 catabolism, collectively elevating HDL-C and improving lipid profiles [51]. The fact that COM ranked just below AE highlights the central role of its aerobic component, suggesting that AE or AE-dominant COM could be prioritized for obese adolescents with low HDL-C.

For LDL-C reduction, HIIT ranked first, followed by RT and COM, while AE was least effective. The underlying mechanisms remain incompletely understood. One noteworthy hypothesis is that HIIT may exert its effects through modulation of certain metabolic hormones. For instance, some studies have shown that HIIT can increase plasma nesfatin levels [52], and observational evidence indicates that nesfatin is negatively correlated with LDL-C. Although this association has not been established as causal, it suggests that nesfatin may be one of the potential mediators of HIIT’s LDL-C–lowering effects. Future studies are needed to directly examine the causal role of nesfatin within exercise interventions. Moreover, COM interventions significantly improved LDL-C and TG levels, consistent with prior findings [31, 53]. It is noteworthy that AE was relatively less effective in improving LDL-C, which may indicate that achieving a significant impact on LDL-C requires AE to reach a specific dose threshold—such as sufficient intensity or cumulative exercise volume. The AE interventions included in this analysis may have varied substantially in these parameters [54], potentially contributing to the weaker observed effects.

In terms of TG improvement, HIIT was most effective, followed by AE and COM, with RT ranking lowest. Elevated TG is common in obese adolescents and is closely associated with hepatic insulin resistance and VLDL-C overproduction. HIIT may improve TG by enhancing hepatic lipid oxidation and reducing intrahepatic fat deposition. In addition, HIIT promotes skeletal muscle fat oxidation, further accelerating TG catabolism and clearance.In adults, HIIT has been shown to reduce VLDL-TG secretion rates independent of weight loss [55]. Although this provides a valuable perspective, we explicitly acknowledge the limitations of applying adult data to this cohort. Given the hormonal and metabolic fluxes unique to puberty, the extent to which these specific pathways operate in adolescents remains to be definitively confirmed.AE ranked second, consistent with RCTs showing that moderate-intensity exercise (2 h at 60% VO₂peak) increased systemic free fatty acid (FFA) flux and fat oxidation, reduced hepatic VLDL-C–apoB-100 secretion, and improved clearance of VLDL-C-TG the following day [56]. This RCT can be considered supportive mechanistic evidence (it was not included in the present NMA).In contrast, RT showed weaker effects on TG, which contradicts much of the existing theory, though findings by Magkos [57], suggest possible explanations: acute moderate bench press exercise (75% 1RM) reduced TG concentrations by enhancing pathways of VLDL-C/TG clearance, including LPL- and hepatic lipase–mediated hydrolysis, lipoprotein conversion (VLDL-C → IDL → LDL-C), and hepatic/peripheral receptor uptake.

This inconsistency suggests that the lipid-regulating effects of RT may follow a pronounced dose–response relationship, with its benefits being highly dependent on training parameters such as intensity and volume. However, the RT protocols used in the pediatric and adolescent studies included in this NMA may not have reached, or consistently maintained, the thresholds necessary to optimize lipid responses.

In addition, the substantial heterogeneity observed in the TG results may have been influenced by multiple factors. Beyond differences in intervention characteristics (e.g., type, intensity, dosage).Beyond intervention differences, we hypothesize that physiological fluctuations in growth hormone secretion during puberty may contribute to this heterogeneity [58]. This remains a postulate requiring verification through future studies stratified by pubertal stage.

Integrating SUCRA rankings across the four lipid outcomes: RT showed advantages in lowering TC and LDL-C; AE was most effective for HDL-C elevation; HIIT excelled in reducing LDL-C and TG; COM, while not superior for any single marker, provided balanced, comprehensive improvements.Taken together, different exercise modalities exhibited distinct patterns of effectiveness across specific lipid outcomes. This suggests that, in practice, intervention strategies may benefit from aligning exercise selection with the predominant dyslipidemia phenotype. For example: For individuals with elevated TC or LDL-C, RT or HIIT may be preferable; For those primarily characterized by low HDL-C, AE may be a more suitable option; For hypertriglyceridemia, HIIT and AE appear to offer more direct improvement; For individuals requiring broad, simultaneous improvement across multiple lipid parameters—or when feasibility is a priority—COM remains a valuable choice.It is crucial to emphasize that SUCRA rankings represent probabilistic ordering and implicitly assign equal weight to all lipid markers, whereas clinical guidelines (e.g., American Heart Association) typically prioritize LDL-C reduction. Therefore, a high statistical ranking in a secondary outcome (e.g., AE for HDL-C) does not imply clinical superiority for patients requiring targeted LDL-C management. These rankings must be interpreted alongside effect sizes and confidence intervals (CIs). Consequently, clinicians should integrate these efficacy rankings with patient-specific lipid phenotypes and clinical priorities rather than applying them as rigid mandates. For instance, while AE ranked first for increasing HDL-C, HIIT ranked first in reducing both LDL-C and triglycerides (TG), carrying greater clinical relevance when the primary objective is to target core cardiovascular risk factors.

Dyslipidemia in pediatric obesity is a major precursor to adult cardiovascular disease, an association confirmed by landmark longitudinal studies (e.g., Bogalusa Heart Study [3], STRIP [59]). While current guidelines broadly recommend 60 min of daily MVPA [59, 60], our findings regarding the distinct lipid-modifying effects of different modalities provide a framework for precision exercise prescriptions. However, integrating these modality-specific recommendations into future clinical guidelines requires research to first prioritize long-term adherence strategies and evaluate their cost-effectiveness relative to general physical activity.

Furthermore, given that the included trials relied on well-resourced settings with the infrastructure for structured interventions (e.g., HIIT, RT), generalizability to resource-limited contexts remains a challenge. Therefore, a critical future direction is translating these modality-specific benefits into context-appropriate, sustainable strategies to ensure equitable improvements in global pediatric metabolic health.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study has several limitations. First, regarding methodological constraints: Assessment of publication bias relied on qualitative visual inspection due to the limited power of formal tests in NMA. Additionally, missing standard deviations were imputed using a fixed correlation coefficient (r = 0.5) without sensitivity analyses; thus, precision estimates should be interpreted with this uncertainty in mind.

Second, regarding biological and clinical heterogeneity: The included participants spanned a broad age range (7–18 years), encompassing different stages of pubertal development. This introduces relevant biological heterogeneity, as hormonal fluctuations during puberty may inherently modulate lipid metabolism and exercise responsiveness. Furthermore, the substantial variability in exercise protocols (e.g., intensity, volume), combined with inconsistent reporting of adherence rates and adverse events across trials, limits our ability to fully assess the real-world feasibility and safety of scaling these interventions.

Third, regarding evidence scope: To isolate exercise effects, we excluded combined diet-exercise interventions, which restricts generalizability to multifaceted clinical settings. Finally, data primarily reflect short- to medium-term effects (≤ 12 months), and some studies utilized adult BMI cutoffs, potentially introducing classification bias regarding obesity status.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study used a network meta-analysis (NMA) to systematically compare the effects of different exercise modalities on the lipid profiles of children and adolescents with obesity. Although most included studies were rated as high quality, we fully accounted for risk of bias when interpreting the findings. For interventions supported by a limited evidence base or imprecise estimates (e.g., wide confidence intervals), we provided more cautious interpretations and did not overstate conclusions simply because the overall study quality was high.

The results indicate that RT appeared most effective for reducing TC, while AE ranked highest for increasing HDL-C. HIIT showed favorable potential for lowering LDL-C and TG; and COM yielded moderate but well-balanced improvements across all lipid parameters. Based on SUCRA probabilities, RT ranked highest for reducing TC, AE for increasing HDL-C, and HIIT for reducing LDL-C and TG. However, these rankings reflect probabilistic trends; given that not all pairwise comparisons reached statistical significance, they should not be interpreted as evidence of absolute superiority. These findings indicate a differentiated pattern of lipid improvements across exercise modalities.

Although not all pairwise comparisons demonstrated definitive statistical superiority, the SUCRA rankings provide probabilistic guidance for prioritizing certain exercise modes depending on the predominant lipid abnormality (e.g., elevated TC, low HDL-C). This differentiated pattern offers preliminary support for developing individualized exercise prescriptions tailored to specific dyslipidemia phenotypes. However, translating these trends into clinical practice will require further research that incorporates patient preferences, long-term adherence, and contextual clinical considerations.

In conclusion, this study confirms that exercise modalities exert differential effects on pediatric lipid profiles. Practically, findings support a phenotype-driven approach: prioritizing HIIT or RT for atherogenic targets (LDL-C/TG) and AE for HDL-C deficits. However, successful translation to practice requires balancing these efficacy rankings with individual feasibility and adherence rather than applying them as rigid mandates.

Accordingly, future research should prioritize: (1) validating the cardiovascular benefits of different exercise modalities in long-term trials; and (2) evaluating their real-world feasibility, adherence, and cost-effectiveness. Accumulation of such evidence will be essential for considering the integration of more modality-specific exercise recommendations into future clinical guidelines.
